title: Ezekiel 47
subtitle: The River of Life and the Boundaries of the Land

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Temple Vision

sections.header: Verses 1-12 — The River Flowing from the Temple

heb.paragraph: Water (mayim) flows from under the threshold of the temple toward the east, emerging south of the altar. As it flows eastward, the guide measures four intervals of a thousand cubits, each time leading Ezekiel through water that progressively deepens: ankle-deep, knee-deep, waist-deep, and finally a river too deep to cross. The exponential deepening without any tributary is miraculous: the water's source is YHWH's presence, and its abundance increases supernaturally. The river transforms the Arabah (the arid Jordan Rift Valley) and heals the Dead Sea, converting salt death into freshwater life.

The verb rapha ('to heal') describes what happens when the river reaches the Dead Sea (yam hammawet, lit. 'sea of death'). The waters are 'healed' (yeraphu), producing abundant fish and supporting lush vegetation along both banks. The trees bear fresh fruit every month, with leaves that serve for healing (terufah). However, the swamps and marshes are not healed — they remain as salt sources, demonstrating that even in the restored creation, utility and purpose govern the extent of transformation.

refs.note: A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, then divided into four rivers. Ezekiel's temple river deliberately echoes this Edenic geography, presenting the new temple as a return to paradise.
A fountain will flow from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Acacias. Joel's eschatological spring parallels Ezekiel's temple river.
Living water will flow from Jerusalem, half to the eastern sea and half to the western sea. Zechariah's vision expands Ezekiel's eastward-only river to both coasts.

The river of the water of life flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb, with trees of life on both sides bearing twelve kinds of fruit monthly, with leaves for the healing of the nations. This is the most direct and extensive adaptation of Ezekiel 47 in the New Testament.
There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God. The psalm likely draws on the same temple-spring tradition that Ezekiel develops into a full cosmic river.

notes.note: Water flows from under the temple threshold, south of the altar. The source is not natural but supernatural: God's presence generates life-giving water. In the millennial kingdom, this literal river will flow from the literal temple in Jerusalem, transforming the geography of the land.

The four measurements show the river deepening without any tributaries. This is miraculous growth: the further from the temple, the deeper the water. God's blessing does not diminish with distance but increases. By the fourth measurement, the river is unfordable — overwhelming in its abundance.

The river flows to the Arabah and heals the Dead Sea. The Dead Sea, at 1,412 feet below sea level and approximately 34% salinity, supports no aquatic life. The healing of this sea is one of the most dramatic geographical transformations in all of prophetic Scripture.

Fishermen will stand along the shore from En Gedi to En Eglaim. These are actual Dead Sea locations, confirming the literal geographical referent. The abundance of fish will rival the Mediterranean. Where death reigned, life will superabound.

The swamps remain salty — salt is still needed for cooking, preservation, and sacrifice. Even in the renewed creation, pragmatic utility is maintained. The trees bearing monthly fruit with healing leaves anticipate Revelation 22:2 and the ultimate consummation.

Calvin reads the river as a figure for the gospel flowing from the church (the true temple) and bringing spiritual life wherever it reaches. The progressive deepening represents the gradual spread of the gospel through the nations. The Dead Sea represents the spiritually dead who are brought to life by the water of God's Word. Calvin explicitly rejects a literal interpretation: the vision communicates the abundance of divine grace, not a hydrological engineering project.

The trees with monthly fruit and healing leaves represent the ongoing, ever-fresh provision of God's grace through the ministry of the Word. Calvin sees this as fulfilled in the church's preaching, which continually produces new fruit and brings healing to wounded souls.

The NET notes that the water emerges from under the threshold (miphtan) of the temple, not from the altar itself. The altar directs the flow southward, but the source is the temple as the dwelling place of God. The imagery reverses the aridity of the Judean wilderness.

The NET identifies 'the sea' (hayyammah) as the Dead Sea, based on the geographical reference to the Arabah. The phrase 'the waters will be healed' (nirpe'u hammayim) uses the niphal passive, emphasizing that the healing is divine action, not natural process.

The NET notes the exception clause regarding swamps and marshes as a realistic limitation within the visionary landscape. Not everything is transformed; salt remains necessary for cultic and domestic purposes.

Block calls this passage 'the most magnificent vision of cosmic renewal in the Old Testament.' He traces its tradition-historical roots to the Edenic river (Gen 2:10), the Zion-spring tradition (Ps 46:4; Joel 3:18), and the cosmic water mythology of the ancient Near East. Block emphasizes three features: (1) the supernatural source — no tributaries, only the temple; (2) the progressive deepening — grace increases with distance; (3) the Dead Sea healing — the most lifeless body of water on earth becomes teeming with life. This is not merely restoration but transformation on a cosmic scale.

Block notes that the monthly fruit-bearing of the trees is botanically impossible under normal conditions, signaling that the vision operates at a level beyond natural law. The leaves 'for healing' (litrufah) anticipate the eschatological elimination of disease and decay. This passage provides the primary source text for Revelation 22:1-2.

Zimmerli identifies the temple-river motif as belonging to the Zion-theology tradition, in which the sacred mountain is the source of cosmic fertility (cf. Ps 46:5; Isa 33:21). He notes that Ezekiel's version is the most fully developed iteration of this tradition in the Hebrew Bible. Zimmerli attributes the passage to a late redactional layer that shares eschatological concerns with Joel 4:18 and Zechariah 14:8. The progressive deepening is a literary-theological construct rather than a topographical description: it illustrates the principle that divine blessing intensifies as it extends.

hist.context: The guide brings Ezekiel back to the temple entrance, where water flows from under the threshold toward the east and south of the altar. The river is measured at four thousand-cubit intervals, deepening supernaturally at each stage from ankle-deep to an unfordable torrent. No tributaries feed it; the temple itself is the sole source. The river flows eastward through the Arabah to the Dead Sea, healing its salt waters and producing such abundance of fish that fishermen line its shores from En Gedi to En Eglaim. Trees line both banks, bearing fruit monthly and producing leaves for healing — a passage that profoundly influenced Revelation 22:1-2. The swamps and marshes are excepted from healing, remaining as salt sources, a realistic detail within the visionary landscape. This river is not merely a water supply but a cosmic image: life flows from God's dwelling place and transforms everything it touches. The barren becomes fertile, the dead becomes living, the salt becomes fresh. It is a reversal of the curse and an anticipation of new creation.

sections.header: Verses 13-23 — The Boundaries of the Promised Land

heb.paragraph: The gevul (border) of the land follows an idealized version of the boundaries promised in Numbers 34:1-12. The northern border runs from the Great Sea (Mediterranean) through Hethlon, Lebo-Hamath, Zedad, Berothah, and Sibraim to Hazer-hatticon on the border of Hauran. The eastern border runs along the Jordan between Hauran/Damascus and Gilead down to Tamar, then to the waters of Meribah-Kadesh. The southern border extends from Tamar to the Wadi of Egypt to the Great Sea. The western boundary is the Great Sea itself. Joseph receives a double portion (two tribal allotments), preserving the traditional Josephite inheritance of Ephraim and Manasseh.

refs.note: The original boundary description for the promised land. Ezekiel's borders broadly correspond but extend further north, reflecting the Davidic empire ideal.
Jacob adopts Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons, giving Joseph a double portion. Ezekiel preserves this tradition by allotting Joseph two tribal territories.
Isaiah promises that foreigners who bind themselves to YHWH will not be excluded. Ezekiel's inclusion of resident aliens in the land distribution parallels this inclusive vision.
Gentiles are no longer foreigners and strangers but fellow citizens and members of God's household. The ger provision in Ezekiel 47:22-23 anticipates the Pauline theology of Gentile inclusion.

notes.note: The land is divided among twelve tribes with Joseph getting a double share. The oath YHWH swore to give this land to the patriarchs is cited as the basis for the allocation. The land promise is not cancelled by the exile; it is reaffirmed and fulfilled in the eschatological restoration.

The boundaries trace an idealized territory that corresponds to the Davidic empire at its height. The northern border extends to the region of Hamath, which Solomon's kingdom briefly encompassed (2 Chr 8:3-4). This is the full extent of the promised land as originally envisioned.

The inclusion of resident aliens in the land distribution is extraordinary. Foreigners who have settled among Israel and raised children receive inheritance rights equal to native Israelites. This is the most inclusive land-distribution text in the Old Testament and foreshadows the incorporation of Gentiles into the people of God.

Calvin interprets the boundaries as representing the fullness of God's promises. The idealized territory is not a cartographic projection but a theological affirmation that God's promises will be fulfilled in their totality. For Calvin, the ultimate 'promised land' is the heavenly inheritance of which earthly Canaan was always a type.

Calvin seizes on the alien-inclusion provision as evidence that God's redemptive purposes always extended beyond ethnic Israel. The ger who receives inheritance foreshadows the Gentile who receives the gospel. Ezekiel's vision, though set in Israelite categories, points to a universal inheritance.

The NET notes that 'Joseph shall have two portions' preserves the traditional double inheritance of the firstborn. Since Levi receives no tribal territory (replaced by the sacred district), the double Joseph portion maintains the twelve-tribe allotment system.

The NET observes that the ger-inclusion provision is without parallel in Pentateuchal land-distribution legislation. The closest parallel is the general command to treat the ger justly (Lev 19:33-34), but Ezekiel goes further by granting actual land inheritance within tribal territories. This represents a significant expansion of covenant community boundaries.

Block notes that the boundary description serves as the geographical frame for the tribal allotments of chapter 48. He emphasizes the ger provision in vv. 22-23 as the most theologically innovative element of the entire land-distribution schema. In a book that has been preoccupied with boundaries between holy and common, clean and unclean, Israelite and foreigner, this provision dramatically widens the definition of 'Israel' to include resident aliens who have committed to life within the community. Block calls it 'one of the most remarkable statements about inclusion in the entire Old Testament.'

Zimmerli identifies this unit as a Grenzbestimmung (boundary determination) that draws on the priestly tradition of Numbers 34 while adapting it to eschatological geography. The extended northern border reflects Second Temple period aspirations for a restored Davidic kingdom. Zimmerli regards the ger-inclusion provision as a late redactional addition reflecting the increasingly cosmopolitan composition of the post-exilic community. Its placement at the end of the boundary description transforms the entire land-distribution from an ethnic to a religious criterion.

hist.context: YHWH specifies the boundaries of the restored land according to the twelve tribes, with Joseph receiving a double portion (thus twelve allotments for thirteen tribes, since Levi receives the sacred district instead of tribal land). The boundaries broadly correspond to the idealized borders of Numbers 34, though with some differences in the northern extent. The northern boundary extends from the Mediterranean through Hethlon to Lebo-Hamath and further to Damascus, encompassing territory that was part of David's empire at its greatest extent. The eastern boundary is the Jordan, running between Gilead and the land of Israel. The southern boundary extends from Tamar through the waters of Meribah-Kadesh to the Wadi of Egypt. The western boundary is the Mediterranean Sea. A remarkable provision concludes the unit: resident aliens (gerim) who live among the tribes and have children are to be allotted inheritance alongside native-born Israelites, within whichever tribe they reside. This inclusion of gerim in the land distribution is unprecedented in Pentateuchal legislation and represents a dramatic expansion of covenant community boundaries.

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: Water Emerges from the Temple Threshold

rows.label: vv.6-8
rows.text: The River Flows to the Arabah and Dead Sea

rows.label: vv.12
rows.text: Trees of Life: Monthly Fruit, Healing Leaves

lit.note: Chapter 47 moves from the cosmic (temple river) to the cartographic (land boundaries), linking divine vitality to territorial restoration. The river section (vv. 1-12) is among the most celebrated passages in prophetic literature.

themes.note: Covenant and Mercy peak as the chapter presents the land promise's fulfillment and the radical inclusion of resident aliens. Sovereignty is high as YHWH determines boundaries and generates the supernatural river. Mission rises through the inclusive vision that anticipates the expansion of God's people beyond ethnic Israel.

hebtext.note: Water (mayim) flows from under the threshold of the temple toward the east, emerging south of the altar. The gevul (border) of the land follows an idealized version of the boundaries promised in Numbers 34:1-12. trans.title: Comparing translations in The River of Life and the Boundaries of the Land

translations.text: He then brought me out through the north gate and led me around the outside to the outer gate facing east, and the water was trickling from the south side.
Then he brought me out by way of the north gate and led me around on the outside to the outer gate that faces toward the east; and behold, the water was trickling out on the south side.
When I arrived there, I saw a great number of trees on each side of the river.
As I went back, I saw on the bank of the river very many trees on the one side and on the other.

debate: The NET notes that the water emerges from under the threshold (miphtan) of the temple, not from the altar itself. The altar directs the flow southward…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The River of Life and the Boundaries of the Land within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.anchor: The River of Life and the Boundaries of the Land
thread.text: There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God. The psalm likely draws on the same temple-spring tradition that Ezekiel develops into a full cosmic river.

thread.anchor: The River of Life and the Boundaries of the Land
thread.text: The original boundary description for the promised land. Ezekiel's borders broadly correspond but extend further north, reflecting the Davidic empire ideal.

coaching.tip: The river flows from the temple threshold, deepening from ankle-deep to swimming-deep in four stages (vv. 3–5). Where it reaches the Dead Sea, the salt water becomes fresh and 'swarms of living creatures will live wherever the river flows' (v. 9). Death reversed by the water of God's presence. John sees this river again in Revelation 22:1–2 — the 'river of the water of life' flowing from God's throne. Ezekiel's river is the Bible's last image of paradise, bookending Eden's rivers in Genesis 2.

The land boundaries (vv. 13–23) include a remarkable provision: 'You are to allot it as an inheritance for yourselves and for the foreigners residing among you. They are to be considered as native-born Israelites' (v. 22). Aliens receive inheritance alongside the tribes. Ezekiel's restored Israel isn't ethnically exclusive — it absorbs outsiders into the covenant community. The vision anticipates Paul's declaration that Gentiles are 'fellow heirs' (Ephesians 3:6).
